chunk_summary: This section outlines the protocol for conducting oral food challenges (OFCs), including dose escalation, stopping criteria, safety measures, and post-challenge recommendations for structured food reintroduction.
learning_objectives: Understand the stepwise protocol for oral food challenges, including dose escalation and monitoring intervals.
Identify objective and subjective stopping criteria for allergic reactions during OFCs.
Learn the minimum safety requirements and emergency preparedness for conducting OFCs.
Recognize the importance of structured food reintroduction following a negative OFC.
key_facts: OFCs typically use 4-dose or 6-dose escalation protocols with doses given at 15–30 minute intervals.
Stopping criteria include generalized urticaria, angioedema, repetitive vomiting, progressive cough, stridor, hypotension, or marked behavioral changes.
Persistent subjective symptoms like itchy throat or abdominal pain require observation and may warrant stopping if they persist beyond 45 minutes or recur across doses.
Minimum safety requirements include trained staff, oxygen, fluids, bronchodilator, antihistamines, IM epinephrine, and an escalation/transfer plan.
Structured reintroduction involves regular ingestion of the food multiple times per week to prevent avoidance due to fear.
reference_quote: Conducting an Oral Food Challenge requires a standardized approach, including pre-calculated emergency medication doses and trained staff presence throughout. (AAAAI Practice Parameter, 2020)
